content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : C. B. Craig
    Commissioner
    California Highway Patrol

Date : December 28, 197[7]

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION - Kenneth W. Goshorn, Research
        Specialist III, Conflicts of Interest Division

Subject: Conflict of Interest Code of the California Highway Patrol/
         Reporting of Certain Monies Received by Designated Employees


This is in answer to your memorandum to Chairman
Lowenstein dated July 25, 1977, and a subsequent exchange
of phone calls and letters between our staff and Mr. Dave
Hutchinson of your staff. The following advice is issued
pursuant to Government Code §83114(b).

You ask whether certain employees designated as
Category A and B employees under the Conflict of Interest
Code of the California Highway Patrol, published in Title
13 of the California Administrative Code, are required to
report reimbursements, provided expenses, or honoraria from
nonprofit organizations or governmental entities. Mr.
Hutchinson has provided us with a list of typical situa-
tions raising the question of reporting obligations. From
his list it appears that the Commissioner, Deputy Commis-
sioner, and the Deputy Chief of the Enforcement Services
Division are the designated employees involved in all or
most all applicable situations. For the purposes of this
memorandum we will assume that the persons were invited
personally and specifically to attend the events in ques-
tion because of their position with the CHP, and not as a
general representative of the Agency.

With reference to the list of events provided by Mr.
Hutchinson, it should first be noted that to the extent
the "reimbursements" are received from a state or local
government agency, they are not "income" within the meanin of Government Code §82030(b)(2) or the relevant def-
initional section of the Conflict of Interest Code.
Similarly, receipt of such monies from the state also
need not be reported under any circumstances. However,

C. B. Craig
December 23, 1977                                                Page 2


     the Code and the Political Reform Act do not exclude
payments from federal sources* from the definition of
"income".

     Employees in Category A must report all "income"
under the Code.  Accordingly, the payments in question
must be disclosed as income, gifts or honoraria depending on their character and the services provided in
return.  Regulation 2 Cal.Adm. Code §18728** (attached)
sets forth the alternative ways of reporting such payments.  Your attention is specifically directed to subsection (a) of the Regulation which provides that certain receipts are not "payments" and, therefore, need
not be reported.  You will note that subsection (a)
applies only to "inter-state" transportation and
"necessary accommodations".  This Regulation would
appear to obviate the need for reporting in many
instances cited in Mr. Hutchinson's letter to Mr.
Prim dated October 12, 1977.

     Employees in Category B of the Code are subject
to more specific and limited disclosure responsibilities.  My reading of subsections (2)(a)-(c) indicates
that the disclosure categories of such employees do
not include payments from the federal government nor
the private nonprofit entities cited in Mr. Hutchinson's letter to Mr. Prim dated October 12, 1977.
Accordingly, employees in Category B need not report
the receipt of such payments under any circumstances.

     I trust this answers your question.  Please
forgive our delay in responding.  If you have further
questions concerning this matter, please feel free to
call on me at 322-6444.


     *Previously, the Commission has unsuccessfully
supported legislation which would have excluded payments from the federal government from the definition
of income.  On several occasions the Commission has
approved Conflict of Interest Codes which expressly
exclude federal payments from their coverage, and
the CHP may wish to consider amending their code to
so provide.  Such an amendment would in all probability be approved by the Commission.

     **The CHP Code incorporates the regulations of
the Commission.

Accordingly, the payments in question must be disclosed as income, gifts or honoraria depending on their character and the services provided in return. Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18728** (attached) sets forth the alternative ways of reporting such payments.
